segments.s592-g17: برفتند is an old past-tense third-person plural verb (bar-raftand) meaning “they went/left.” The order is V S1 و S2 بهم (verb first, then the coordinated subjects), a common poetic inversion: "برفتند نعمان و منذر بهم" = "Nuʿmān and Munḏir departed together." بهم here modifies the verb, meaning “together/with one another.”
segments.s592-g18: همه تازیان یمن بیش و کم is a noun phrase in which همه ("all/everyone") quantifies تازیان ("the Arabs"); یمن is a genitival/specifier ("of Yemen"). بیش و کم is an idiomatic pairing meaning “more and less / great and small,” placed after the noun for emphasis.
segments.s592-g19: همه زار و با شاه گریان شدند has predicate-final verb شدند (they became/they were), with زار functioning as an adjectival/participial descriptor (“woeful”/“lamenting”) and با شاه marking accompaniment (“with the king”). گریان (weeping) is a predicative adjective/participle linked to شدند: “they became weeping / they wept.”
segments.s592-g20: ابی آتش از درد بریان شدند is a poetic clause: ابی (lit. “watery/of water”) qualifies آتش ("fire") as a metaphor for tearful eyes; از introduces the cause (“from/because of”), درد is emotional pain/grief here, and بریان شدند is a compound predication meaning “were seared/burnt/consumed” (figuratively consumed by sorrow). The verb is again past plural شدند.
segments.s592-g21: زبان برگشادند زان پس ز بند: زبان ("tongue/speech") is the direct object/subject of برگشادند (they opened/revealed) — an idiom meaning “they spoke” or “they opened their mouths.” زان پس (“after that/thereupon”) is a temporal connector; ز بند is an ablative phrase “from restraint/constraint,” explaining their change of state before speaking. Word order is flexible/poetic (verb before or after constituents).
segments.s592-g22: که‌ای here functions as a vocative formula (classical 'که ای' ≈ “O”), introducing direct speech. پرهنر is an epithet ("much-skilled/cultured"), شهریار is the addressee/title ("king/sovereign"), and بلند is an epithet meaning “lofty/high.” The phrase is a reverential direct address: “O learned, lofty sovereign.”
segments.s592-g23: همه در جهان خاک را آمدیم: همه (all) is the subject; در جهان خاک را is a prepositional/locative-accusative phrase (“into/in the world’s dust”) with را marking the definite object/ground, and آمدیم is first-person plural past (“we came/have come”). The clause expresses the human condition poetically: 'we all came into the world's dust.'
segments.s592-g24: نه جویای تریاک را آمدیم is a negated parallel clause: نه (not) negates the following participial phrase جویای (seeking), تریاک را is the object ("opium" with object marker را), and آمدیم repeats the first-person plural past “we came.” Word-for-word: “we did not come as seekers of opium.” The construction contrasts purpose/intent with the previous existential clause.
segments.s592-g1: پس (then) opens the clause with a temporal/conclusive particle. آگاهی آمد is a noun (آگاهی, 'news/information') + past-tense verb آمد ('came/arrived'); the indirect object is marked by به + name: به بهرام گور = 'to Bahram Gūr'. Note گور here is an epithet (onager) attached to the name.
segments.s592-g2: که introduces a reported-content clause ('that ...'). از چرخ شد is a short idiom: از + چرخ (the heavens/fate) + شد (became/happened), so 'by/from the wheel of fate it happened'. تخت را uses را as the definite-object marker (here marking تخت 'the throne'), and آب شور is a nominal phrase 'salty water' describing the state the throne fell into; the verb شد scopes over تخت را آب شور (i.e. 'the throne became salty water').
segments.s592-g3: پدرت = پدر + -ت is 'your father' (second-person suffix). آن سرافراز شاهان is a phrase of praise ('that lofty/illustrious of kings'); بمرد here functions as a past narrative verb 'died' (classical usage where the b- prefix appears on verbs), so the whole clause reads 'Your father, that lofty one among kings, died.'
segments.s592-g4: به مرد is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner of a man / like a man' (i.e. manly or fitting death). و links to the next clause: همه نام شاهی ببرد is a verbless-to-verb sequence where ببرد (present/imperfect-form of برد used in narrative) means 'carried off/took away' and نام شاهی = 'the renown/name of kingship'. So: 'and he carried away all the renown of kingship.'
segments.s592-g5: یکی مرد = 'a certain man' (یکی used as 'a certain'). بر گاه بنشاندند is verb-final: بر گاه ('on the dais/throne') + بنشاندند (they seated/installed) — a transitive past plural 'they set (him) upon the platform'.
segments.s592-g6: به شاهی = 'to kingship' (marks the office/role). همی is an archaic verbal particle common in Ferdowsi indicating an ongoing or immediate action (here part of the narrative style). خواندند here means 'they called/named/proclaimed' (they called him by the name/title), so the clause means 'they proclaimed him king, calling him Khosrow.'
segments.s592-g7: بخوردند سوگند یکسر سپاه is an inversion common in poetry: بخوردند سوگند literally 'they took/swore an oath' (بخوردند + سوگند), with یکسر سپاه ('the whole army' or 'the entire host') as the actor; word order places the verb before the actor for emphasis. The phrase means 'the whole army swore an oath.'
segments.s592-g8: کزان تخمه is a relative/prepositional phrase 'from which seed/lineage'; هرگز نخواهیم شاه is a negated future-ish construction meaning 'we will never (نخواهیم) [want/allow/have] a king.' Put together: 'that from that stock/seed we will never have a king.'
segments.s592-g65: همه is the subject 'everyone' placed before the verb for emphasis. تاختن is a verbal noun 'the charge/attack' and is marked as a definite object by را. بیاراستند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb (archaic poetic form of بی‌آراستن) meaning 'they arrayed/arranged/armed.' The whole clause literally: 'Everyone arrayed the attack.'
segments.s592-g66: به + noun (به بیدادی) here functions adverbially: 'with cruelty / cruelly.' از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (their) place(s).' برخاستند is past, 3rd‑person plural 'they rose, they sprang up.' The clause thus means 'they rose from their places in cruelty/with violence.'
segments.s592-g67: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when/since.' از تخم is a genitive expression 'from the seed/stock' meaning 'of the lineage.' شاهنشهان is the genitive plural 'of the shahanshahs.' کس نبود is a negative existential: کس 'no one' + نبود (past of بودن) 'there was not.' Altogether: 'When there was no one from the shahanshahs' line.'
segments.s592-g68: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' referring back to کس. یارست is an archaic copular/adjective phrase built on یار (here 'ally/supporter') meaning 'was a friend/supporter.' تخت کیی را is the object phrase 'the Kayanian throne' (تخت = throne, کیی = Kayanian), marked by را. بسود is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to the advantage of / for the profit of.' The clause: 'who was a supporter of the Kayanian throne to its advantage.'
segments.s592-g69: به ایران is a prepositional phrase 'to/against Iran.' همی is the archaic/poetic particle that gives a continuous or emphatic sense ('indeed/so did'); it often appears before the subject. هرکسی is the indefinite subject 'each person / whoever.' دست آخت is an idiomatic, archaic verbal phrase: دست 'hand/power' + آخت (an older past form) meaning 'laid hands on / attacked / seized at.' The clause says 'each person laid hand on Iran' (i.e., strove to seize it).
segments.s592-g70: به شاهنشهی is a prepositional phrase 'for the imperial/kingship.' تیز گردن is a compound epithet, literally 'sharp‑necked' (figuratively 'eager, forward, ready to thrust forward one’s neck'); it modifies the actors. فراخت is a past verb (archaic form) meaning 'they stretched forth / thrust forward' (here: strained toward, sought). Together the clause conveys 'they, with eager/keen necks, reached for the imperial throne' — i.e., they vied ruthlessly for kingship.
segments.s592-g49: من is the first‑person subject 'I'. ایرانیان را is the definite/direct‑object phrase ('the Iranians') marked by را; the object precedes the verb which is common in poetic word order. نمایم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of 'to show' used here with a future/volitional sense ('I will show'). که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'who/that...', and شاه stands as the predicate noun inside that relative clause with the copula (است) omitted, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s592-g50: کدامست is a contracted classical form of کدام است meaning 'which/which one is' and functions as the interrogative/predicate inside the relative clause. با introduces attributes or accompaniment ('with'), followed by a coordinated list تاج و گنج و سپاه ('crown and treasure and army') as the attributes that identify the king. The order places the interrogative verb before the prepositional attribute phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s592-g51: بیاورد is a past (narrative) form 'he brought' (third person); in poetry the verb can appear before the subject, which we see here. نعمان is the proper name 'Nuʿmān' (the subject). سپاهی گران is the object phrase 'a heavy/formidable army' where the adjective گران follows the noun, a normal Persian adjective placement in literary style.
segments.s592-g52: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone' modifying the following coordinated nominal phrase. تیغ‌داران and نیزه‌وران are agentive plural compounds ('sword‑bearers' and 'spearmen') joined by و; they are in apposition to سپاهی گران (they specify the composition of the host). There is no verb here because these nouns are connected to the verb in the previous segment.
segments.s592-g53: بفرمود is a polite/past narrative verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause ('that...'). تاختنها is a pluralized form derived from the infinitive 'تاختن' (literally 'the chargers/those who charge'), formed as a noun by adding ها; برند is the present/subjunctive 3rd plural of بردن used after تا to express what should happen ('they should go/charge'). Together this segment expresses the ordered action.
segments.s592-g54: همه is 'all/everyone'. روی کشور is a locative phrase 'over/upon the surface of the country' (روی functioning as 'on/over' here). به پی is an idiomatic manner phrase 'by foot / in pursuit on foot' where به marks the manner ('by') and پی means 'foot; track' (here 'on foot' / 'in pursuit'). بسپرند is the present/subjunctive 3rd plural of سپردن used in the commanded clause ('they should set/commit [the pursuit]'), completing the injunction begun with تا in the previous segment.
segments.s592-g55: ره شورستان is a genitive noun phrase 'the road/way of the tumult' (ره 'way' governing شورستان). تا here means 'as far as' indicating extent. در طیسفون is the locative 'in/into Ctesiphon'. The whole segment states the extent of the tumultuous road reaching the city.
segments.s592-g56: زمین خیره شد is a nominal + adjectival result: زمین 'the earth/ground' + خیره شد 'became astonished/was made to stare' (شد as past 'became' or auxiliary), a poetic way to say the ground was struck/stunned. زیر نعل اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'beneath the horseshoe(s) of the interior/within' with زیر 'under' followed by نعل 'horseshoe' and اندرون 'within/inside' specifying location; the phrase explains what caused the earth's state.
segments.s592-g25: بمیرد is the classical/poetic optative/indicative form of مردن with the prefixed ب- (here rendered as 'may/let die' or 'shall die'), placed before the patient; کسی 'someone' is the indefinite subject, کو is the archaic/poetic relative particle (= که, 'who'), and ز مادر بزاد is a relative clause 'who was born of a mother' with بزاد (past of زادن) meaning 'was born.' The clause order is verb — indefinite — relative — origin, typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s592-g26: زهش (= ازِ او, 'from/of him') is a contracted prepositional phrase indicating source or of whom; چون here functions temporally/causally ('when' / 'since') introducing the condition; ستم is the object 'injustice/oppression'; بینم is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I see'; و links to the result clause, and مرگ داد is a verb+object sequence where مرگ (death) precedes داد (gave/gives) — 'I deal/give death.' The sequence therefore reads 'when I see injustice from him, I give him death.'
segments.s592-g27: به منذر is the indirect object 'to Mundhir' (preposition به + name); چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the third-person past 'said' (classical narrative past) and the subject بهرام گور ('Bahram Gur') comes at the end for emphasis — a common poetic word order: '[To Mundhir] thus said Bahram Gur.'
segments.s592-g28: که introduces the reported clause ('that'); اکنون 'now' sets time; چو + شد here gives a temporal sense 'when/now that ... became' (چو شد = 'when it became'); روز ما 'our day' is the subject, and تار و تور is an idiomatic paired phrase (literal warp/cord and net) meaning 'tangled/ruined' or 'mired/confused.' The clause therefore states 'that now, when our day has become tār o tū r...' (i.e. things have gone awry).
segments.s592-g29: ازین is the fused preposition+demonstrative (از + این) 'from this'; تخمه‌گر here refers to 'this seed/stock' or 'breed' (a poetic term for lineage); نام 'name' (i.e. reputation/title) and شاهنشهی 'of kingship/empire' together form the genitive phrase 'the name of kingship.' The whole phrase marks the thing (the royal name) that will suffer.
segments.s592-g30: گسسته شود is a passive/resultative construction 'will be broken/come apart' (گسسته = 'broken', شود = 'become'), followed by بگسلد (an active verb 'will rend/break apart') and فرهی ('the noble/renowned' — here the noble stock or aristocratic element). The two verbs give a double image: the royal name will be broken and the noble class will be torn asunder (word order is inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s592-g31: ز دشت 'from the plain' gives the source; سواران 'the horsemen' is the agent; برآرند is a third-person plural present/optative form 'they lift/bring up' (poetic subjunctive), and خاک is the direct object 'dust/earth' — 'the horsemen will raise dust from the plain.'
segments.s592-g32: شود is the impersonal/passive 'will become'; جای 'place/lot' governs بر تازیان 'for the Arabs' (تازیان = Arabs), and بر مغاک 'on/into the abyss' gives the destination or doom. The clause thus states 'the Arabs' place will become the abyss,' with the prepositional phrase at the end for emphasis.
segments.s592-g33: پراندیشه ('full of thought') is an adjective used predicatively as an exhortation; باشیـد is the 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive of بودن 'to be' here meaning 'be (plural/formal)'. The coordinating و links this clause to the following command; یاری کنـید is a compound verb where یاری is the object/noun 'help' and کنید (imperative) is the verbal imperative 'do/provide', so the phrase means 'and give help'.
segments.s592-g34: به marks the beneficiary/target 'for' or 'over' and introduces the phrase 'به مرگ پدر' = 'for (the) death of (the) father'. سوگواری is a noun 'mourning', and کنید is again the 2nd-person plural imperative 'perform/do', so the whole clause is an imperative: 'mourn (for) the father's death.' Word order follows Persian: preposition + noun, then noun object, then verb.
segments.s592-g35: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; the sequence places the source first: 'from Bahram'. بشنید is the past tense 3rd‑person singular 'he heard'. منذر is the (proper) name and functions as subject, while سخن is the thing heard ('speech/words'). The order is somewhat inverted but yields 'Munḏir heard speech from Bahram.'
segments.s592-g36: به introduces the indirect object 'to/for a man'; مردی یکی = 'a certain man' (مردی = 'a man', یکی = 'one / a certain'), with یکی emphasizing indefiniteness. پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply' and افگند is the past tense verb 'he cast/uttered'. بن follows the verb as a short poetic particle meaning roughly 'down/forth' (here reinforcing the sense of casting/uttering), so the clause means 'he cast/uttered an answer to a certain man.'
segments.s592-g37: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت functioning here as 'said'); کاین is the contracted classical form of 'که این' meaning 'that this' or 'this/that which', and روزگار is 'time' or 'fate'. منست is an archaic/copular form (classical contraction) meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' or more generally 'suits/is appropriate to me' in context — together: 'Thus he said that this time/fate is his.'
segments.s592-g38: برین is 'بر + این' = 'on this'; Persian often writes the elements together in poetry. دشت = 'plain' and روز شکار = 'day of the hunt' (روز = 'day', شکار = 'hunt'/'quarry'), with منست again the copula meaning 'is (mine/appropriate)'. The clause literally reads 'On this plain, the day of hunting is mine,' i.e. 'this plain is where today I hunt / today is my hunting day.'
segments.s592-g39: تو is the 2nd-person singular/plural pronoun 'you'; بر تخت = 'on (the) throne' (بر + تخت). بنشین is an imperative 'sit down/sit (you)', and نظاره باش is a linked imperative where نظاره (a verbal noun meaning 'observation/watch') combines with باش (imperative of بودن) to mean 'keep watch' or 'be observing'. The two imperatives are connected by و 'and'.
segments.s592-g40: همه‌ساله is an adverbial 'each year / yearly'. با introduces the instruments/associates: با تاج 'with a crown' and با یاره 'with companions/attendants'; و links the two. باش is again the imperative 'be' so the clause exhorts 'be yearly with crown and with companions' — idiomatically: 'each year wear the crown and be accompanied by followers.'
segments.s592-g41: Nominal phrase: 'همه' (all/everyone) is the quantifier for the head noun 'نامداران' (the renowned). 'برین' is the older, contracted form of 'بر این' (literally 'on this' = 'here/at this place'); it is formed from the preposition 'بر' + the enclitic/variant 'ین' (این). 'هم‌سخن' is the predicate/adjective meaning ‘of the same speech’ / ‘like‑minded’ and follows the noun phrase in the typical Persian order (subject → predicate).
segments.s592-g42: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or content. 'نعمان و منذر' is a coordinated subject (two proper names). 'فگندند' is a classical past 3rd‑person plural (فَکندند → they threw/cast); the prefix/orthography is archaic (فـ instead of modern فَ). The noun 'بن' (literally 'root, foundation') is the direct object of فگندند here; the clause therefore says they 'cast down/uprooted' the foundation (figurative).
segments.s592-g43: Prepositional phrase + verb: 'ز' (poetic from) + 'پیش' (before/in front of) form 'from before' and modify the following agent or scene. 'جهانجوی' is a compound noun ('world‑seeker', i.e. an ambitious person) that functions as the referent. 'برخاستند' is the past 3rd‑person plural of برخواستن (to rise, to stand up/leave) and completes the clause.
segments.s592-g44: 'همه' again quantifies the actors. 'تاختن' is a verbal noun meaning 'the charge/charging' (here an action prepared for). The particle 'را' marks that verbal noun as a definite/direct object/topic. 'بیاراستند' is the past 3rd‑person plural of بیاراستن (to array/prepare/arm); in poetic usage it means they ‘made ready’ or ‘dressed’ themselves for the charge.
segments.s592-g45: 'بفرمود' is the narrative/honorific past (he said/commanded); in courtly narrative it introduces reported speech. 'منذر' is the subject (the speaker). 'به نعمان' marks the indirect object/recipient 'to Nuʿmān'. 'که' here introduces the content of the order (a reported imperative). 'رو' is the imperative 'go' (2nd person singular) in classical Persian; that meaning (imperative) is not listed among the lexical senses provided, so it is treated as an imperative form rather than the nominal senses shown.
segments.s592-g46: 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite/numeral modifier meaning 'one' or 'a single' (i.e. 'one force'). 'لشکری' is the object 'an army' (adjectival/attributive form of لشکر). 'ساز' is an imperative verb here ('make/form/raise') — the dictionary senses given are nominal, so the verbal imperative use is not among those senses (hence -1). 'شیران' is a metaphorical plural noun 'lions' meaning brave fighters; 'نو' modifies it as 'new'. The order is: numeral/quantifier + noun + verb(imperative) + modifier phrase.
segments.s592-g47: A list of origins: 'ز' (from) + 'شیبان' (here a tribal/proper name rather than the literal 'the grey‑haired') and 'و' (and) + 'از' (from) + 'قیسیان' (the Qaysīs, likewise a tribal/proper name). The numeral phrase 'ده هزار' (ten thousand) is the quantity taken from those groups. Word order places the sources before the quantity.
segments.s592-g48: 'فرازآر' functions as an agent/name (literally 'uplifter' or a leader’s epithet; here treated like a proper name). 'گرد' is used as a verbal element meaning 'gathered/turned/got together' — in classical diction گرد can function as a finite verb form (the lexical senses include 'to go about'). 'از در کارزار' is a locative phrase 'from the gate/entrance of the battle' or 'at the field of battle' (ز/از + در + کارزار). Overall the segment says this commander gathered them at / from the battlefield entrance.
segments.s592-g9: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that' / 'who') linking this clause to the previous sentence. Word order is subject after the subordinating conjunction: بهرام (proper name) is the subject, فرزند او = 'his son' is a genitival noun phrase ('فرزند' + او = 'his son'), همچو اوست is a predicate: همچو + pronoun + ست (poetic/collapsed form of است) = 'is like him.' ست is an archaic/poetic contraction of the copula است.
segments.s592-g10: از introduces a source ('from'); آب here is used figuratively (the father's 'water' = his essence/lineage), پدر is the possessor ('father'). یافت is a past-tense verb 'obtained/received' with the postposed subject او ('he' — i.e. Bahram) following the verb, and مغز و پوست are the direct objects ('brain and skin' = figuratively intelligence and appearance). The construction is idiomatic: 'from the father's water he obtained X.'
segments.s592-g11: چو here means 'when' (temporal). بشنید is past tense 'he heard'; the logical subject is بهرام, which follows the verb in poetic word order ('when Bahram heard'). رخ را is the definite object marked by را ('his face'), and بکند is a poetic/past form of the verb (here from کردن/کندن) meaning 'he tore/struck/ruined' — i.e. a violent gesture of mourning. The clause thus reads 'When Bahram heard, he rent his face.'
segments.s592-g12: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'because of' or 'from'; مرگ پدر = 'the death of the father' (genitive). شد is the past/auxiliary 'became,' دلش = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش = 'his'), and مستمند is an adjective meaning 'afflicted/needy' — here 'his heart became afflicted/sorrowful.' The clause gives the emotional consequence of the death.
segments.s592-g13: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth.' دو هفته is a duration ('two weeks'); in Persian duration phrases commonly follow or precede the verb in poetry. ز شهر یمن 'from the city of Yemen' is the source phrase (ز = from, شهر = city, یمن = Yemen). Together: 'For two weeks there came forth from the city of Yemen...'.
segments.s592-g14: خروشیدن is the infinitive/noun-like verbal form 'to cry out/roar/rise in an outcry' — here used as the event that 'arose.' The following series کودک و مرد و زن lists those making the cry ('children and men and women'). The phrase functions as the object/description of what 'arose' from the city.
segments.s592-g15: چو = 'when'; یک ماه بنشست literally 'one month sat' — a poetic way to say 'a month had passed/elapsed.' با سوک شاه = 'with the king's mourning' (با + سوک = 'in mourning'), so the whole clause means 'when a month of the king's mourning had elapsed.' بنشست is an older/poetic past form meaning 'sat/settled' used idiomatically for the passing of time.
segments.s592-g16: سر ماه نو = 'the start (head) of the new month' (سر = start/top, ماه نو = new month). را is the object marker and بیاراست is the verb 'he adorned/made ready' (past); گاه as a noun means 'time/occasion' and here reinforces that a particular occasion/time (the month's start) is being prepared/celebrated. The clause therefore says he arranged/celebrated the beginning of the new month.
segments.s592-g57: A simple past clause: the enumerated nouns زن و کودک و مرد (women, children, and men) are the direct objects listed before the verb; بردند is 3rd-person plural past of بردن 'to take/carry' and marks the action by an unspecified plural agent ('they'). اسیر follows the verb as a resultative/object complement meaning 'captive' (literally ‘they took ... captive’). Word order (objects before verb) is common in poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s592-g58: An existential negative clause: کس ... نبد = 'no one was' (نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past, contraction of نبود). آن رنجها را is the definite/affected phrase ('those sufferings') marked by را as the object/thing concerned. دستگیر here is a predicate noun/adjective 'a helper/succorer', so literally 'no one was a helper of those sufferings' → 'no one could help with those troubles.'
segments.s592-g59: General predicative clause: پر از + noun means 'full of ...'. غارت و سوختن are a coordinated nominal phrase ('plunder and burning' — سوختن here a verbal noun/gerund 'burning'). شد is the past of شدن 'to become'. جهان is the subject following the verb (verb–subject order appears in poetry): 'the world became full of plunder and burning.'
segments.s592-g60: A temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو 'when'. بیکار شد is 'became empty/idle' (بیکار used here = 'vacant/without an occupant'), and تخت شاهنشهان is a genitival phrase 'the throne of the shahanshahs'. Together: 'when the shahanshahs' throne became vacant.'
segments.s592-g61: Simple clause: پس 'then' sets the sequence; آگاهی آمد is 'information/news came/arrived' with آمد past of آمدن. به marks destination/target, so به روم و به چین = 'to Rum and to China.' Repetition of به is stylistic emphasis in listing places.
segments.s592-g62: Continuation of the destination list: repeated preposition به 'to' before each place (ترک, هند, مکران) names where the news spread; زمین after مکران is the generic noun 'land/land of' forming 'the land of Makran'. This is a simple coordination of locations.
segments.s592-g63: Subordinate clause introduced by که 'that/for': شد is past 'became', تخت ایران is the construct 'throne of Iran', ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and خسرو is the name Khosrow. تهی is the adjective 'empty' or 'devoid (of)'. Together: 'for the throne of Iran became empty of Khosrow.'
segments.s592-g64: An existential negative statement: کسی نیست 'there is no one' (کسی here carries the negative sense in combination with نیست). زیبای شاهنشهی is a nominal phrase where زیبای functions as an adjective/noun 'beautiful/fit one' modifying or qualifying شاهنشهی 'of imperial kingship' (i.e., 'one fit for/beautiful for the imperial kingship'). The whole yields 'there is no one worthy/fit for the imperial throne.'

sentences.s592-s9: Nuʿmān and Munḏir went off together, with all the Arabs of Yemen, great and small.
sentences.s592-s10: They all bewailed and wept with the king; their tear-filled eyes were seared by grief.
sentences.s592-s11: They unsealed their tongues; then, loosed from restraint, they said, 'O learned and lofty sovereign!'
sentences.s592-s12: We have all come into the dust of the world; we did not come seeking opium.
sentences.s592-s1: Then news came to Bahram Gūr that by the wheel (i.e. the heavens/fate) the throne had become salty water.
sentences.s592-s2: Your father, that proud among kings, died like a man, and carried off all the renown of kingship.
sentences.s592-s3: They seated a certain man upon the dais/throne, and proclaimed him king, calling him Khosrow.
sentences.s592-s4: The whole army swore an oath that from that seed we will never have a king.
sentences.s592-s33: They all arrayed the charge, and with cruelty rose up from their places.
sentences.s592-s34: Since there was no one from the line of the shahanshahs who could be a supporter of the Kayanian throne to its advantage.
sentences.s592-s35: To Iran, indeed everyone laid hands upon it, and each stretched forward his keen neck toward the imperial throne (i.e., vied for the kingship).
sentences.s592-s25: I will show the Iranians who the king is, with crown, treasure, and army.
sentences.s592-s26: Nuʿmān brought a mighty host — all sword‑bearers and spearmen.
sentences.s592-s27: He commanded that the horsemen charge forth, and that all, across the face of the country, set out in pursuit on foot.
sentences.s592-s28: The road of tumult reached into Ctesiphon; the ground within stood aghast beneath the horseshoes.
sentences.s592-s13: Let any man die who was born of a mother; when I see him do injustice, I give him death.
sentences.s592-s14: Bahram Gur said thus to Mundhir: 'Now that our day has become tār and tū r.'
sentences.s592-s15: From this stock the name of kingship will be broken; the noble line will be rent apart.
sentences.s592-s16: From the plain the horsemen will raise dust; for the Arabs their place will be the abyss.
sentences.s592-s17: Be thoughtful and give help; mourn (for) your father's death.
sentences.s592-s18: Munḏir heard words from Bahram; he cast an answer to a certain man.
sentences.s592-s19: Thus he said, 'This time (or fate) is mine; on this plain it is my day for the hunt.'
sentences.s592-s20: You sit upon the throne and keep watch; every year be with crown and with companions.
sentences.s592-s21: All the renowned men here were of one mind, for Nuʿmān and Munḏir had cast down the foundation.
sentences.s592-s22: They rose up from before the world‑seeker, and all arrayed themselves for the charge.
sentences.s592-s23: Munḏir commanded Nuʿmān, 'Go — form an army, make a band of new lions.'
sentences.s592-s24: From the Shaybān and from the Qaysīs ten thousand (men); Farazâr gathered them from the gate of battle.
sentences.s592-s5: that Bahram, his son, resembled him; from his father's water he obtained brain and skin.
sentences.s592-s6: When Bahram heard, he tore his face; at his father's death his heart became afflicted.
sentences.s592-s7: For two weeks there arose from the city of Yemen the outcry of child and man and woman.
sentences.s592-s8: When a month of the king's mourning had passed, he prepared the beginning of the new month.
sentences.s592-s29: They carried off women and children and men as captives; no one was there to help those sufferings.
sentences.s592-s30: The world became full of plunder and burning when the throne of the shahanshahs stood empty.
sentences.s592-s31: Then news arrived at Rum and at China — and to the Turks and to India and to the land of Makran.
sentences.s592-s32: For the throne of Iran became empty of Khosrow; there is no one fit for the imperial kingship.
